On this page

`SELECT` allows you to specify a list of columns and expressions to be selected
and evaluated from a table.

tip

Looking for SELECT best practices? Checkout our
[**Maximize your SQL efficiency: SELECT best practices**](https://questdb.com/blog/2024/03/11/sql-select-statement-best-practices/)
blog.

## Syntax[​](#syntax "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the SELECT keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Note: `table` can either a specified table in your database or passed forward as
the result of a sub-query.

## Simple select[​](#simple-select "Direct link to Simple select")

### All columns[​](#all-columns "Direct link to All columns")

QuestDB supports `SELECT * FROM tablename`. When selecting all, you can also
omit most of the statement and pass the table name.

The two examples below are equivalent

QuestDB dialect[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=trades%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
trades;
```

Traditional SQL equivalent[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20*%20FROM%20trades%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT * FROM trades;
```

### Specific columns[​](#specific-columns "Direct link to Specific columns")

To select specific columns, replace \* by the names of the columns you are
interested in.

Example:

Select specific columns[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%20side%20FROM%20trades%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT timestamp, symbol, side FROM trades;
```

### Aliases[​](#aliases "Direct link to Aliases")

Using aliases allow you to give expressions or column names of your choice. You
can assign an alias to a column or an expression by writing the alias name you
want after that expression.

note

Alias names and column names must be unique.

Column aliases[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20price%20AS%20rate%2C%0A%20%20%20%20amount%20quantity%0AFROM%20trades%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT timestamp, symbol,  
    price AS rate,  
    amount quantity  
FROM trades;
```

Notice how you can use or omit the `AS` keyword.

### Arithmetic expressions[​](#arithmetic-expressions "Direct link to Arithmetic expressions")

`SELECT` is capable of evaluating multiple expressions and functions. You can
mix comma separated lists of expressions with the column names you are
selecting.

Arithmetic expressions[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20timestamp%2C%20symbol%2C%0A%20%20%20%20price%20*%200.25%20AS%20price25pct%2C%0A%20%20%20%20amount%20%3E%2010%20AS%20over10%0AFROM%20trades%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT timestamp, symbol,  
    price * 0.25 AS price25pct,  
    amount > 10 AS over10  
FROM trades;
```

The result of `amount > 10` is a boolean. The column will be named "over10" and
take values true or false.

## Boolean expressions[​](#boolean-expressions "Direct link to Boolean expressions")

Supports `AND`/`OR`, `NOT` & `XOR`.

### AND and OR[​](#and-and-or "Direct link to AND and OR")

AND returns true if both operands are true, and false otherwise.

OR returns true if at least one of the operands is true.

```prism-code
SELECT  
    (true AND false) AS this_will_return_false,  
    (true OR false) AS this_will_return_true;
```

### NOT[​](#not "Direct link to NOT")

NOT inverts the truth value of the operand.

```prism-code
SELECT  
    NOT (true AND false) AS this_will_return_true;
```

### XOR[​](#xor "Direct link to XOR")

^ is the bitwise XOR operator. It applies only to the Long data type.
Depending on what you need, you might prefer to cast the input and
output to boolean values.

```prism-code
SELECT  
    (1 ^ 1) AS will_return_0,  
    (1 ^ 20) AS will_return_21,  
    (true::int ^ false::long)::boolean AS will_return_true,  
    (true::int ^ true::long)::boolean AS will_return_false;
```

## Aggregation[​](#aggregation "Direct link to Aggregation")

Supported aggregation functions are listed on the
[aggregation reference](/docs/query/functions/aggregation/).

### Aggregation by group[​](#aggregation-by-group "Direct link to Aggregation by group")

QuestDB evaluates aggregation functions without need for traditional `GROUP BY`
whenever there is a mix of column names and aggregation functions
in a `SELECT` clause. You can have any number of discrete value columns and
any number of aggregation functions. The three statements below are equivalent.

QuestDB dialect[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20symbol%2C%20avg(price)%2C%20count()%0AFROM%20trades%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT symbol, avg(price), count()  
FROM trades;
```

Traditional SQL equivalent[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20symbol%2C%20avg(price)%2C%20count()%0AFROM%20trades%0AGROUP%20BY%20symbol%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT symbol, avg(price), count()  
FROM trades  
GROUP BY symbol;
```

Traditional SQL equivalent with positional argument[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20symbol%2C%20avg(price)%2C%20count()%0AFROM%20trades%0AGROUP%20BY%201%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT symbol, avg(price), count()  
FROM trades  
GROUP BY 1;
```

### Aggregation arithmetic[​](#aggregation-arithmetic "Direct link to Aggregation arithmetic")

Aggregation functions can be used in arithmetic expressions. The following
computes `mid` of prices for every symbol.

Aggregation arithmetic[Demo this query](https://demo.questdb.io/?query=SELECT%20symbol%2C%20(min(price)%20%2B%20max(price))%2F2%20mid%2C%20count()%20count%0AFROM%20trades%3B&executeQuery=true)

```prism-code
SELECT symbol, (min(price) + max(price))/2 mid, count() count  
FROM trades;
```

tip

Whenever possible, it is recommended to perform arithmetic `outside` of
aggregation functions as this can have a dramatic impact on performance. For
example, `min(price/2)` is going to execute considerably more slowly than
`min(price)/2`, although both return the same result.

## Supported clauses[​](#supported-clauses "Direct link to Supported clauses")

QuestDB supports the following standard SQL clauses within SELECT statements.

### CASE[​](#case "Direct link to CASE")

Conditional results based on expressions.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-1 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of CASE](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[CASE reference](/docs/query/functions/conditional/)

### CAST[​](#cast "Direct link to CAST")

Convert values and expression between types.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-2 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the CAST keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[CAST reference](/docs/query/sql/cast/)

### DISTINCT[​](#distinct "Direct link to DISTINCT")

Returns distinct values of the specified column(s).

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-3 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the DISTINCT keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[DISTINCT reference](/docs/query/sql/distinct/).

### FILL[​](#fill "Direct link to FILL")

Defines filling strategy for missing data in aggregation queries. This function
complements [SAMPLE BY](/docs/query/sql/sample-by/) queries.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-4 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the FILL keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[FILL reference](/docs/query/sql/fill/).

### JOIN[​](#join "Direct link to JOIN")

Join tables based on a key or timestamp.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-5 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the high-level syntax of the JOIN keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[JOIN reference](/docs/query/sql/join/)

### PIVOT[​](#pivot "Direct link to PIVOT")

Transforms rows into columns by aggregating data across specified values.
Useful for analytics, charting, and reshaping time-series data.

For more information, please refer to the
[PIVOT reference](/docs/query/sql/pivot/)

### LIMIT[​](#limit "Direct link to LIMIT")

Specify the number and position of records returned by a query.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-6 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the LIMIT keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[LIMIT reference](/docs/query/sql/limit/).

### ORDER BY[​](#order-by "Direct link to ORDER BY")

Orders the results of a query by one or several columns.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-7 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the ORDER BY keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[ORDER BY reference](/docs/query/sql/order-by/)

### UNION, EXCEPT & INTERSECT[​](#union-except--intersect "Direct link to UNION, EXCEPT & INTERSECT")

Combine the results of two or more select statements. Can include or ignore
duplicates.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-8 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the UNION, EXCEPT &amp; INTERSECT keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[UNION, EXCEPT & INTERSECT reference](/docs/query/sql/union-except-intersect/)

### WHERE[​](#where "Direct link to WHERE")

Filters query results

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-9 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the WHERE clause](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

QuestDB supports complex WHERE clauses along with type-specific searches. For
more information, please refer to the
[WHERE reference](/docs/query/sql/where/). There are different syntaxes for
[text](/docs/query/sql/where/#symbol-and-string),
[numeric](/docs/query/sql/where/#numeric), or
[timestamp](/docs/query/sql/where/#timestamp-and-date) filters.

## Additional time-series clauses[​](#additional-time-series-clauses "Direct link to Additional time-series clauses")

QuestDB augments SQL with the following clauses.

### LATEST ON[​](#latest-on "Direct link to LATEST ON")

Retrieves the latest entry by timestamp for a given key or combination of keys
This function requires a
[designated timestamp](/docs/concepts/designated-timestamp/).

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-10 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the LATEST ON keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[LATEST ON reference](/docs/query/sql/latest-on/).

### SAMPLE BY[​](#sample-by "Direct link to SAMPLE BY")

Aggregates [time-series data](https://questdb.com/blog/what-is-time-series-data/) into homogeneous time chunks. For example daily
average, monthly maximum etc. This function requires a
[designated timestamp](/docs/concepts/designated-timestamp/).

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-11 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the SAMPLE BY keyword](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, please refer to the
[SAMPLE BY reference](/docs/query/sql/sample-by/).

### TIMESTAMP[​](#timestamp "Direct link to TIMESTAMP")

Dynamically creates a
[designated timestamp](/docs/concepts/designated-timestamp/) on the output of a
query. This allows to perform timestamp operations like [SAMPLE BY](#sample-by)
or [LATEST ON](#latest-on) on tables which originally do not have a designated
timestamp.

caution

The output query must be ordered by time. `TIMESTAMP()` does not check for order
and using timestamp functions on unordered data may produce unexpected results.

#### Syntax[​](#syntax-12 "Direct link to Syntax")

![Flow chart showing the syntax of the timestamp function](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For more information, refer to the
[TIMESTAMP reference](/docs/query/functions/timestamp/)
